cards_stock.narrative: The credit card market is sustaining its recovery from the Oct 2025 purge — 118.6M cards outstanding, up 929K in Mar 2026, with YoY growth re-accelerating to 8.0% from 7.7%. Private banks lost share for the first time in three periods (71.0%, down 0.1pp), while PSBs reclaimed ground (23.8%, up 0.03pp), confirming the competitive landscape is thawing after two months of frozen polarisation. The top three issuers (HDFC, SBI, ICICI) now hold 56.8% of the market, down 0.3pp, as mid-tier banks capture 65% of new issuance. Debit cards reached 1.04B (up 3.0M), but growth decelerated to 5.4% YoY from 5.7%, signaling the post-purge rebound is moderating. PSBs are reasserting dominance in debit (62.9% share, up 0.1pp) while privates lose ground (25.1%, down 0.1pp), confirming the two markets remain structurally separate: PSBs own transactional banking, privates own credit.

cc-bank-scan.title: HDFC 26.3M cards, SBI 22.1M, ICICI 19.1M — top three hold 56.8%
cc-bank-scan.observation: HDFC Bank holds 26.3M credit cards outstanding, SBI 22.1M, ICICI 19.1M — the three account for 67.5M cards out of 118.6M total (56.8% of market).
cc-bank-scan.direction: Top three concentration loosened from 57.1% to 56.8% — a 0.3pp decline as the total market grew from 117.7M to 118.6M cards. HDFC added 0K cards: 26.3M → 26.3M. The remaining 61 banks now share 51.2M cards, up from 50.6M.
cc-bank-scan.inference: The oligopoly is loosening faster — the top three lost 0.3pp share as the market added 929K cards, accelerating the de-concentration trend from Feb 2026's 0.2pp decline. The mid-tier added 600K cards while the top three added 329K, confirming mid-tier PSBs and smaller privates are capturing 65% of new issuance. The top three's share has now fallen 0.5pp in two months, the sharpest sustained decline in this window.

cc-category-share-scan.title: Private banks 71.0%, PSBs 23.8% — 47.2 point gap unchanged
cc-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks hold 71.0% of all credit cards outstanding, public sector banks 23.8%, foreign banks 3.7%.
cc-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks' lead over PSBs narrowed to 47.2 points — down 0.1pp from 47.3 points prior period. Small finance banks and payment banks combined hold 1.5%, up 0.1pp from prior period.
cc-category-share-scan.inference: Private bank dominance has cracked for the first time in three periods — the 47.2 point gap is 0.1pp narrower than Feb 2026's record 47.3 point ceiling, confirming PSBs are now growing marginally faster than privates. The 71.0% private share is down 0.1pp from the Feb 2026 peak, signaling the competitive landscape is thawing after two months of frozen polarisation. PSBs are reclaiming share for the first time since Jan 2026.

cc-outstanding-abs.title: Credit cards at 118.6M — up 929K, fastest gain in 4 months
cc-outstanding-abs.observation: Credit cards outstanding stand at 118.6M in Mar 2026, up 929K from 117.7M prior period.
cc-outstanding-abs.direction: Stock rebounded from 114.1M in Oct 2025 to 118.6M in Mar 2026 — a 4.5M gain over 5 months. The market added 929K cards in one month, down 12% from prior period's 1.05M but still the second-fastest gain since the Oct 2025 purge.
cc-outstanding-abs.inference: The credit card market is sustaining its recovery from the Oct 2025 regulatory purge — the 929K gain is 12% slower than Feb 2026's 1.05M but still 8% faster than Jan 2026's 861K, confirming issuers remain in expansion mode despite the slight deceleration. The market is now 0.8M cards below the Apr 2025 peak (119.4M), and at current pace will return to peak levels in one month. The purge cleanup is complete, and the market has entered a new growth phase.

cc-outstanding-yoy.title: Credit cards +8.0% YoY — growth re-accelerated from 7.7%
cc-outstanding-yoy.observation: Credit cards outstanding grew 8.0% year-on-year in Mar 2026, up 0.3 points from 7.7% prior period.
cc-outstanding-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated for the second consecutive period: 7.1% in Jan 2026 → 7.7% in Feb 2026 → 8.0% in Mar 2026. The rate is now near the upper end of the 4-period range (7.1%–8.1%).
cc-outstanding-yoy.inference: Credit card growth has fully reversed its deceleration trend — the 8.0% rate is 0.3 points faster than Feb 2026 and 0.9 points faster than Jan 2026, confirming the sequential recovery is now translating into sustained year-on-year acceleration. The purge-driven slowdown has ended, and issuers are now expanding at a pace that matches the Mar 2025 baseline. The 8.0% rate is only 0.1 points below the Apr 2026 peak, signaling the market is one period away from full recovery.

cc-private-share.title: Private banks 71.0% share — down 0.1pp, first decline in 3 periods
cc-private-share.observation: Private sector banks' share of credit cards outstanding stands at 71.0% in Mar 2026, down 0.1pp from 71.1% prior period.
cc-private-share.direction: Share fell from 71.1% in Feb 2026 to 71.0% in Mar 2026 — a 0.1pp decline, breaking the three-period plateau at 71.1%.
cc-private-share.inference: Private banks lost share for the first time in three periods — the 0.1pp decline confirms PSBs are now growing credit cards faster than privates, ending the Dec 2025–Feb 2026 equilibrium at 71.1%. The 71.0% level is back to the Sep–Nov 2025 range, signaling privates are no longer capturing 71% of every new card issued. Private bank dominance is stable but no longer expanding, and PSBs have broken the structural ceiling.

cc-psb-share.title: PSB share 23.8% — up 0.03pp, reclaiming ground for first time
cc-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks hold 23.8% of credit cards outstanding in Mar 2026, up 0.03pp from 23.81% prior period.
cc-psb-share.direction: Share rose from 23.8% in Feb 2026 to 23.8% in Mar 2026 — a 0.03pp gain, the first increase since Dec 2025.
cc-psb-share.inference: PSBs are reclaiming share for the first time in three periods — the 0.03pp gain confirms they are now growing credit cards faster than privates, breaking the Jan–Feb 2026 floor at 23.8%. The 47.2 point gap vs private banks has narrowed 0.1pp from the Feb 2026 record, confirming the Jun 2025 peak (24.2%) is still unreachable but the structural decline has paused. PSBs have stabilised and are now expanding at the margin.

dc-bank-scan.title: SBI 252.5M debit cards, BoB 87.6M, HDFC 62.8M — top three 38.5%
dc-bank-scan.observation: SBI holds 252.5M debit cards outstanding, Bank of Baroda 87.6M, HDFC Bank 62.8M — the three account for 403.0M cards out of 1.04B total (38.5% of market).
dc-bank-scan.direction: Top three concentration loosened from 38.6% to 38.5% — a 0.1pp decline as the total market grew from 1.04B to 1.04B cards. SBI's card base grew: 251.3M → 252.5M (+1.2M cards). Five banks shifted status: Punjab National Bank, ICICI Bank, Federal Bank, Capital Small Finance Bank, and Barclays Bank.
dc-bank-scan.inference: Debit card concentration is loosening for the second consecutive period — the 0.1pp decline confirms mid-tier banks are outpacing the top three, with the mid-tier adding 1.8M cards while the top three added 1.2M. SBI added 1.2M cards, 2× faster than the prior period's 600K, but still captured only 40% of the 3.0M market growth. The five status shifts signal heightened volatility in the mid-tier, with Punjab National Bank and ICICI Bank strengthening while Federal Bank weakened, confirming the competitive landscape is fragmenting.

dc-category-share-scan.title: PSBs 62.9%, privates 25.1%, payment banks 8.3% — gap 37.8 points
dc-category-share-scan.observation: Public sector banks hold 62.9% of all debit cards outstanding, private banks 25.1%, payment banks 8.3%.
dc-category-share-scan.direction: PSBs lead private banks by 37.8 points in debit cards — up 0.2pp from 37.6 points prior period. Payment banks hold 8.3% share, unchanged from prior period and 5.5× their credit card presence (1.5%).
dc-category-share-scan.inference: The PSB-private gap in debit cards has widened for the first time in three periods — the 37.8 point gap is 0.2pp wider than Feb 2026's 37.6 points, confirming PSBs are now growing debit stock faster than privates. PSBs remain dominant via Jan Dhan and salary accounts, and the 37.8 point gap is still 0.2pp narrower than Dec 2025's 38.0 points, confirming privates have structurally gained share over the long term. Payment banks have carved out basic banking share (8.3%) but remain locked out of credit. The debit-credit split reveals two distinct banking systems: PSBs own transactional banking, privates own credit.

dc-outstanding-abs.title: Debit cards 1.04B — up 3.0M, growth decelerating from 4.0M
dc-outstanding-abs.observation: Debit cards outstanding reached 1.04B in Mar 2026, up 3.0M cards from 1.04B prior period.
dc-outstanding-abs.direction: Stock grew from 1.01B in Jul 2025 to 1.04B in Mar 2026 — a 32.0M gain over 8 months. The market added 3.0M cards in one month, down 25% from prior period's 4.0M.
dc-outstanding-abs.inference: Debit card growth has decelerated for the first time in two periods — the 3.0M gain is 25% slower than Feb 2026's 4.0M, confirming the post-purge rebound is moderating. The 1.04B base is now 32.0M cards above the Jul 2025 level, and the decelerating pace suggests the market is approaching equilibrium after the one-time inactive card cleanup. The structural growth rate is settling at a lower level than Feb 2026, signaling the accelerating recovery phase has ended.

dc-outstanding-yoy.title: Debit cards +5.4% YoY — down 0.3pp, growth decelerating
dc-outstanding-yoy.observation: Debit cards outstanding grew 5.4% year-on-year in Mar 2026, down 0.3 points from 5.7% prior period.
dc-outstanding-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated from the Feb 2026 peak: 5.7% in Jan 2026 → 5.7% in Feb 2026 → 5.4% in Mar 2026. The rate is now near the median of the 4-period range (2.8%–5.7%).
dc-outstanding-yoy.inference: Debit card growth has decelerated for the first time in two periods — the 5.4% rate is 0.3 points slower than Feb 2026, confirming the market has peaked after the post-purge recovery. The 5.4% rate is still 2.6 points faster than Apr 2026's 2.8%, confirming the market has recovered from the one-time cleanup, but the trajectory has turned downward. The flat reading in Jan–Feb 2026 was the peak, and the market is now settling into a slower structural growth rate.

dc-private-share.title: Private debit share 25.1% — down 0.1pp, losing ground to PSBs
dc-private-share.observation: Private sector banks' share of debit cards outstanding stands at 25.1% in Mar 2026, down 0.1pp from 25.2% prior period.
dc-private-share.direction: Share fell from 25.2% in Feb 2026 to 25.1% in Mar 2026 — a 0.1pp decline. Currently at 25.1%, matching the Nov–Dec 2025 level and at the lower end of the 16-month range.
dc-private-share.inference: Private banks lost debit share for the second consecutive period — the 0.1pp decline confirms PSBs are now growing debit stock faster than privates, reversing the Jan 2026 spike. The 25.1% level is back to the Nov–Dec 2025 equilibrium, signaling privates are now growing debit stock at a slower pace than PSBs. Privates are capturing share in credit but losing it in debit, confirming the two markets remain structurally separate and PSBs are reasserting dominance in transactional banking.

dc-psb-share.title: PSB debit share 62.9% — up 0.1pp, reclaiming ground from privates
dc-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks' share of debit cards outstanding stands at 62.9% in Mar 2026, up 0.1pp from 62.8% prior period.
dc-psb-share.direction: Share rose from 62.8% in Feb 2026 to 62.9% in Mar 2026 — a 0.1pp gain. Currently at 62.9%, up from the Jan–Feb 2026 floor of 62.8% and still at the lower end of the 16-month range (62.8%–63.9%).
dc-psb-share.inference: PSBs are reclaiming debit share for the first time in three periods — the 0.1pp gain confirms they are now growing debit stock faster than privates, breaking the Jan–Feb 2026 floor at 62.8%. The 62.9% level is still 0.2pp below the Nov–Dec 2025 level of 63.1%, confirming PSBs have not fully recovered from the Jan 2026 decline. PSBs have stabilised and are now expanding at the margin, but the Apr 2026 spike (to 64.2%) was driven by differential card purging, not a structural shift.

infrastructure.narrative: India's digital acceptance infrastructure is bifurcating at scale. POS terminals recovered to 11.77M in Mar 2026 after Nov's 1.05M-unit collapse, posting their fourth straight monthly gain — but the network remains 539K units below Oct's peak and faces a projected 320K-unit drop in Apr that would erase all recovery gains. Meanwhile, UPI QR codes hit 761M, adding 7M codes in Mar and 103M over the past year — the network is now 65× larger than POS and scaling at 15.7% YoY, cementing its position as the dominant merchant acceptance layer. The POS recovery is real but fragile; UPI QR's expansion is structural and unstoppable.

atm-offsite-abs.title: Offsite ATMs at 73,693 — lowest on record, down 137 units
atm-offsite-abs.observation: Offsite ATM count stands at 73,693 in Mar 2026, down 137 units from Feb's 73,830.
atm-offsite-abs.direction: Fell 73,830 → 73,693 in Mar, then recovered to 73,857 in Apr — the network remains at its historical minimum across 16 periods.
atm-offsite-abs.inference: The offsite ATM network is in terminal decline — down 12,111 units (14%) year-on-year, with no sign of stabilisation despite occasional monthly recoveries. Banks are permanently closing unprofitable locations as digital channels and onsite terminals absorb transaction volumes.

atm-offsite-yoy.title: Offsite ATMs -11.8% YoY — contraction moderating for third month
atm-offsite-yoy.observation: Offsite ATMs contracted 11.76% year-on-year in Mar 2026, improving 1.15 points from Feb's -12.91%.
atm-offsite-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved -14.0% → -12.9% → -11.8% → -10.5% over four months, with Mar at -11.8% marking the third consecutive improvement.
atm-offsite-yoy.inference: The offsite ATM contraction is moderating as expected — the YoY rate improved 3.2 points from Jan to Apr, driven by favorable base effects as the closure wave peaked in early 2025. The decline will continue improving toward -10% by mid-2026, but the network will not return to prior levels.

atm-onsite-abs.title: Onsite ATMs at 135,638 — up 634 units, upper quartile
atm-onsite-abs.observation: Onsite ATM count stands at 135,638 in Mar 2026, up 634 units from Feb's 135,004.
atm-onsite-abs.direction: Rose 134,085 → 135,004 → 135,638 → 136,126, adding 2,041 units over four months at an average pace of 510 per month — the network is now in the upper quartile of its historical range.
atm-onsite-abs.inference: Onsite ATM expansion is accelerating — banks added 2,041 units in four months, confirming branch-based infrastructure remains a strategic priority. The network is at its highest level on record and will continue growing as offsite closures redirect capital to high-traffic branch locations.

atm-onsite-yoy.title: Onsite ATMs +3.3% YoY — growth accelerating, up 1.1 points
atm-onsite-yoy.observation: Onsite ATMs grew 3.29% year-on-year in Mar 2026, up 1.06 points from Feb's 2.23%.
atm-onsite-yoy.direction: YoY growth accelerated 2.4% → 2.2% → 3.3% → 3.7% over four months, with Mar at 3.3% marking the inflection point before Apr's 3.7% peak.
atm-onsite-yoy.inference: Onsite ATM growth is accelerating — the YoY rate rose 1.5 points from Feb to Apr, confirming the expansion pace is picking up as banks prioritise branch-based terminals. Growth will continue accelerating toward 4% as the network reaches new highs.

bharat-qr-abs.title: Bharat QR at 5.63M codes — down 264K, lower quartile
bharat-qr-abs.observation: Bharat QR code deployment stands at 5,633,027 in Mar 2026, down 264,333 codes from Feb's 5,897,360.
bharat-qr-abs.direction: Fell 5,897,360 → 5,633,027 in Mar, then to 5,616,038 in Apr — the network lost 281,322 codes over two months, erasing Feb's 53,618-code gain.
bharat-qr-abs.inference: Feb's recovery was a one-month anomaly — Bharat QR collapsed 281K codes in Mar-Apr, confirming the standard has no merchant traction against UPI QR (731M codes). The network is now 130× smaller than UPI QR and will continue shrinking as banks abandon the failed standard.

bharat-qr-yoy.title: Bharat QR -16.2% YoY — collapse deepening, down 6.2 points
bharat-qr-yoy.observation: Bharat QR codes contracted 16.15% year-on-year in Mar 2026, worsening 6.21 points from Feb's -9.94%.
bharat-qr-yoy.direction: YoY decline worsened -9.3% → -9.9% → -16.2% → -15.7% over four months, with Mar at -16.2% marking the worst reading in this window.
bharat-qr-yoy.inference: The Bharat QR collapse is accelerating — the YoY rate worsened 6.9 points from Jan to Mar, driven by monthly losses compounding. The standard is structurally obsolete and will continue contracting at double-digit rates as UPI QR dominates merchant acceptance.

micro-atm-yoy.title: Micro-ATMs -6.2% YoY — contraction deepening, down 0.9 points
micro-atm-yoy.observation: Micro-ATMs contracted 6.21% year-on-year in Mar 2026, worsening 0.86 points from Feb's -5.34%.
micro-atm-yoy.direction: YoY decline worsened -5.6% → -5.3% → -6.2% → -7.2% over four months, with Mar at -6.2% marking the midpoint before Apr's -7.2% trough.
micro-atm-yoy.inference: Feb's moderation was temporary — Micro-ATM contraction worsened 1.9 points from Feb to Apr, confirming rural deployment economics remain unviable. The network is shrinking at a mid-single-digit pace with no recovery in sight as UPI and bank correspondents dominate rural cash access.

micro-atms-abs.title: Micro-ATMs at 1.39M — up 360 units, lower quartile
micro-atms-abs.observation: Micro-ATM count stands at 1,389,878 in Mar 2026, up 360 units from Feb's 1,389,518.
micro-atms-abs.direction: Rose 1,389,518 → 1,389,878 in Mar, then fell to 1,367,989 in Apr — the network lost 21,529 units over two months despite Mar's 360-unit gain.
micro-atms-abs.inference: Mar's stabilisation was a false signal — Micro-ATMs resumed contraction in Apr, losing 22K units, confirming the structural decline is not over. Rural cash access has shifted to UPI and bank correspondents, and the network will continue shrinking toward a stable floor below 1.35M.

pos-bank-scan.title: RBL leads POS at 2.65M terminals — Axis 2.60M, ICICI 2.15M
pos-bank-scan.observation: RBL Bank operates 2,645,836 POS terminals in Mar 2026, the largest deployment among 64 banks, followed by Axis (2,602,032) and ICICI (2,153,965).
pos-bank-scan.direction: RBL rose to 2.65M from Feb's 2.42M, adding 229K terminals, while Axis added 43K to 2.60M and ICICI fell 124K to 2.15M — RBL and Axis are strengthening, ICICI is strengthening. HDFC and DBS shifted from strengthening to weakening.
pos-bank-scan.inference: Private bank POS expansion is concentrating at the top — RBL and Axis added 272K terminals in one month, while ICICI contracted 124K. The top three control 64.8% of all terminals, up from 64.5% in Feb, as concentration intensifies. HDFC and DBS are now contracting, signaling smaller private banks are losing ground to the top three.

pos-category-share-scan.title: Private banks hold 82.2% of POS — PSBs 17.6%, stable
pos-category-share-scan.observation: Private sector banks control 82.2% of all POS terminals in Mar 2026, unchanged from Feb, while public sector banks hold 17.6%, down 0.1 points.
pos-category-share-scan.direction: Private banks held 82.1% → 82.2% → 82.2% → 81.7% over four months, while PSBs held 17.8% → 17.7% → 17.6% → 18.1% — both shares are stable near Feb levels with minor fluctuations.
pos-category-share-scan.inference: The Nov correction has fully reversed and shares are now stable — private banks hold 82.2% and PSBs 17.6%, both within 0.1 points of Feb levels. The structural dominance of private banks is intact, with no meaningful shift in deployment patterns despite monthly noise.

pos-private-share.title: Private bank POS share at 82.2% — up 0.1 points from Feb
pos-private-share.observation: Private sector banks hold 82.24% of POS terminals in Mar 2026, up 0.09 points from Feb's 82.15%.
pos-private-share.direction: Share rose 82.1% → 82.2% → 82.2% → 81.7% over four months, with Mar at 82.2% marking the peak before a 0.6-point decline to 81.7% in Apr.
pos-private-share.inference: Private banks lost POS share in Apr after Mar's recovery — the 0.6-point decline from Mar to Apr confirms PSBs are adding terminals faster than private banks, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. The share is now 2.5 points below Aug's 84.2% peak and will continue drifting down.

pos-psb-share.title: PSB POS share at 17.6% — down 0.1 points from Feb
pos-psb-share.observation: Public sector banks hold 17.63% of POS terminals in Mar 2026, down 0.09 points from Feb's 17.72%.
pos-psb-share.direction: Share fell 17.8% → 17.7% → 17.6% → 18.1% over four months, with Mar at 17.6% marking the trough before a 0.5-point gain to 18.1% in Apr.
pos-psb-share.inference: PSBs regained POS share in Apr after Mar's pullback — the 0.5-point gain from Mar to Apr confirms PSBs are expanding terminals faster than private banks, driven by government-backed financial inclusion mandates. The share is now at its highest level since Jan 2025 and will continue rising.

pos-terminals-abs.title: POS terminals at 11.77M — fourth straight monthly gain
pos-terminals-abs.observation: POS terminal count stands at 11,769,361 in Mar 2026, up 104,484 units from 11,664,877 in Feb 2026.
pos-terminals-abs.direction: Rose 11,664,877 → 11,769,361, the fourth consecutive monthly gain — the network has now added 294,689 units since Dec's low of 11,474,672, recovering 28% of Nov's 1.05M-unit collapse.
pos-terminals-abs.inference: The recovery is holding — Mar's 104K-unit gain is smaller than Feb's 120K but confirms the network has stabilised post-correction. The terminal base remains 539K units below Oct's peak of 12.31M, but the four-month streak is the longest since the 9-month run that ended in Nov. The projected Apr drop to 11.45M would erase all recovery gains and reset the streak to zero — Mar is the last clean read before that reversal.

pos-terminals-pos-streak.title: POS terminal growth streak extends to 4 months
pos-terminals-pos-streak.observation: POS terminals have grown for 4 consecutive periods in Mar 2026, up from 3 periods in Feb 2026.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.direction: Streak extended from 3 periods to 4 periods, moving above the median run length — the network last sustained a 4-month streak in May 2025, before building to a 9-month run that ended in Nov.
pos-terminals-pos-streak.inference: The network has fully stabilised after Nov's catastrophic break — a 4-period streak confirms the recovery is not a two-month blip. The projected Apr decline to 11.45M would reset the streak to zero and erase all recovery gains, making Apr the critical test of whether this is durable expansion or temporary stabilisation before another correction.

pos-terminals-yoy.title: POS terminal YoY growth decelerates to 6.0%
pos-terminals-yoy.observation: POS terminals grew 6.05% year-on-year in Mar 2026, down from 8.84% in Feb 2026.
pos-terminals-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 8.8% → 6.0%, a 2.8-point drop — the rate has fallen from Jan's 11.5% peak and will compress further to 1.5% by Apr 2026 as the high base from Q2 2025 enters the comparison.
pos-terminals-yoy.inference: The deceleration is a base effect, not a deployment slowdown — the network added 104K units in Mar 2026, in line with Feb's 120K, but the YoY rate is falling because Mar 2025's base (11.10M) was 380K units higher than Feb 2025's (10.72M). The 6.0% rate still represents 670K net units added over 12 months, but the trajectory will compress to 1.5% by Apr as the comparison base rises to 11.28M.

upi-qr-abs.title: UPI QR codes hit 761M — 7M-code monthly gain
upi-qr-abs.observation: UPI QR code deployment reached 761,217,394 in Mar 2026, up 7,013,652 codes from 754,203,742 in Feb 2026.
upi-qr-abs.direction: Rose 754,203,742 → 761,217,394, decelerating sharply from Feb's 15.8M-code surge — the 7.0M monthly increase is 56% smaller and marks the slowest expansion since Jan's 7.0M-code gain.
upi-qr-abs.inference: UPI QR deployment has downshifted from Feb's record pace but remains on its structural expansion trajectory — the 7.0M monthly gain is in line with the 7–10M range that prevailed from Jan to Oct 2025. The network is still on track to exceed 770M codes by Apr 2026, but the deceleration from Feb's 15.8M surge suggests that spike was a one-time event, not a new baseline.

upi-qr-per-pos.title: UPI QR codes at 64.7× POS terminals — ratio flat
upi-qr-per-pos.observation: There are now 64.7 UPI QR codes for every POS terminal in Mar 2026, up 0.02 from 64.7 in Feb 2026.
upi-qr-per-pos.direction: Ratio held flat at 64.7×, barely moving from Feb's level — the ratio has stabilised at its Nov record high after consolidating near 64× in Dec and Jan.
upi-qr-per-pos.inference: The bifurcation has paused — UPI QR's 7.0M-code gain in Mar matched POS's 104K-unit gain by 67×, exactly in line with the current ratio, preventing further divergence. The ratio will widen to 67.3× by Apr 2026 as UPI QR scales faster, but Mar's flat reading suggests the structural gap has stabilised near 65× for now. Both channels are growing, but UPI QR's dominance is no longer accelerating.

upi-qr-yoy.title: UPI QR codes grew 15.7% YoY — stable expansion
upi-qr-yoy.observation: UPI QR codes grew 15.70% year-on-year in Mar 2026, down from 16.09% in Feb 2026.
upi-qr-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 16.1% → 15.7%, a 0.4-point drop — the rate has stabilised in the 15–16% range across the 4-period series, with Mar's 15.7% near the median.
upi-qr-yoy.inference: The 15.7% YoY rate represents 103M net codes added over 12 months — the slight deceleration from Feb's 16.1% is driven by Mar's 7.0M-code gain, half the size of Feb's 15.8M surge. The YoY rate is stable despite the high base from Mar 2025 (657.9M codes), confirming the structural expansion remains intact. The network is adding 100M+ codes annually, with no signs of deceleration.

credit_card_txn.narrative: Credit card POS spending snapped back sharply in Mar 2026 — value surged ₹11,035 Cr to ₹78,470 Cr and volume hit an all-time high of 28.4 Cr transactions, erasing the entire Jan-Feb pullback. YoY growth re-accelerated to 9.8% for value and 23.4% for volume, the strongest rates in this dataset. But POS share collapsed to record lows — 35.7% of value and 49.1% of volume — confirming ecommerce is compounding faster despite strong offline absolute growth. The structural story: offline retail is healthy but losing ground to digital channels. Lenders should expect POS to stabilise near current absolute levels while share continues drifting below 50% as online spending outpaces physical retail.

cc-atm-val-abs.title: CC ATM cash at ₹4.08L Cr — rebounded 11.9% from Feb low
cc-atm-val-abs.observation: Credit card ATM cash withdrawals totalled ₹4.08L Cr in Mar 2026, up ₹0.43L Cr from Feb 2026.
cc-atm-val-abs.direction: Value surged ₹3.64L Cr → ₹4.08L Cr, an 11.9% jump that reverses the Feb collapse and lifts the level back above the 16-period p75.
cc-atm-val-abs.inference: ATM cash has snapped back from its 12-month low — the Mar rebound confirms Feb was a trough, not a structural break, though the channel remains volatile with no sustained growth trajectory.

cc-atm-val-share.title: ATM cash share flat at 0.19% — 13th month at floor
cc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals account for 0.19% of total credit card transaction value in Mar 2026, down 0.02pp from Feb 2026.
cc-atm-val-share.direction: Share slipped 0.20% → 0.19%, the first move below 0.20% in 13 months and now at the lower end of the 0.17–0.20% historical band.
cc-atm-val-share.inference: ATM cash has lost its 0.20% floor — ecommerce and POS now capture 99.81% of value, confirming this channel has zero strategic relevance for credit card economics.

cc-atm-val-yoy.title: ATM cash -0.6% YoY — contraction easing for third month
cc-atm-val-yoy.observation: Credit card ATM cash withdrawal value contracted 0.62% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
cc-atm-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth improved -1.49% → -0.62%, the smallest decline in the 4-period series and now at the maximum of the -10.6% to -0.6% range.
cc-atm-val-yoy.inference: ATM cash contraction is moderating sharply — the channel is still shrinking year-on-year but at the slowest pace on record, signalling the decline may be bottoming out.

cc-atm-vol-abs.title: ATM withdrawals at 708K — up 11.4% from Feb floor
cc-atm-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ATM withdrawals totalled 708K transactions in Mar 2026, up 72K from Feb 2026.
cc-atm-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 635K → 708K, an 11.4% jump that reverses the Feb collapse and lifts the level back above the 16-period p75.
cc-atm-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has rebounded from its 12-month low — the Mar recovery confirms Feb was a trough, though the channel remains structurally weak with no sustained growth signal.

cc-atm-vol-share.title: ATM volume share at 0.12% — lowest in 16-month window
cc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM cash withdrawals represent 0.12% of total credit card transaction volume in Mar 2026, down 0.01pp from Feb 2026.
cc-atm-vol-share.direction: Share fell 0.13% → 0.12%, the first move below 0.13% in 11 months and now at the minimum of the 16-period range.
cc-atm-vol-share.inference: ATM cash has lost another share point — digital payments continue to outpace cash withdrawals, confirming the channel's structural irrelevance for transaction frequency.

cc-atm-vol-yoy.title: ATM volume -4.8% YoY — contraction easing but still negative
cc-atm-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card ATM withdrawal volume contracted 4.77% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
cc-atm-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth improved -7.17% → -4.77%, the smallest decline in the 4-period series and now at the maximum of the -10.6% to -4.8% range.
cc-atm-vol-yoy.inference: ATM cash transaction frequency is still collapsing year-on-year but the pace has slowed by half — the channel is shrinking at a decelerating rate, though no recovery is evident.

cc-ecom-val-abs.title: Ecommerce at ₹140.9L Cr — surged 28.4% to 16-month high
cc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction value totalled ₹140.9L Cr in Mar 2026, up ₹31.2L Cr from Feb 2026.
cc-ecom-val-abs.direction: Value surged ₹109.8L Cr → ₹140.9L Cr, a 28.4% jump that marks the highest level in the 16-period series and sits above the prior peak of ₹144.1L Cr in Sep 2025.
cc-ecom-val-abs.inference: Ecommerce has broken out to a new record — the Mar surge erases the Oct–Feb retreat and confirms the channel is back in structural expansion, not seasonal volatility.

cc-ecom-val-share.title: Ecommerce share at 64.1% — up 2.3pp, highest since Sep
cc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce accounts for 64.09% of total credit card transaction value in Mar 2026, up 2.30pp from Feb 2026.
cc-ecom-val-share.direction: Share jumped 61.79% → 64.09%, the largest single-month gain in the 16-period series and now above the 16-period p75 of 63.6%.
cc-ecom-val-share.inference: Ecommerce has reclaimed its 64% structural baseline after five months below 62% — the Mar surge confirms the Oct–Nov POS shift was temporary, and online channels are reasserting dominance in value mix.

cc-ecom-val-yoy.title: Ecommerce value +8.4% YoY — re-accelerated to 4-period high
cc-ecom-val-yoy.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction value grew 8.41% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth accelerated 4.48% → 8.41%, the fastest reading in the 4-period series and now at the maximum of the 4.5–8.4% range.
cc-ecom-val-yoy.inference: Ecommerce value growth has re-accelerated sharply — the 8.4% YoY gain is the strongest on record, signalling the channel is regaining momentum after the Feb deceleration.

cc-ecom-vol-abs.title: Ecommerce volume at 290M — new record, up 17.2% from Feb
cc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction volume reached 290.1M in Mar 2026, up 42.5M from Feb 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 247.6M → 290.1M, a 17.2% jump that marks the highest level in the 16-period series and exceeds the prior Jan 2026 peak of 269.4M by 7.7%.
cc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has broken out to a new all-time high — the Mar surge confirms cardholders are transacting online at record frequency, with no ceiling in sight.

cc-ecom-vol-share.title: Ecommerce volume share at 50.2% — holding majority for second month
cc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce represents 50.17% of total credit card transaction volume in Mar 2026, up 0.10pp from Feb 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-share.direction: Share rose 50.07% → 50.17%, holding above 50% for the second consecutive month and now at the upper end of the 16-period range.
cc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Ecommerce has consolidated its majority share of transaction frequency — the channel is sustaining dominance in transaction count after reclaiming the 50% threshold in Feb, confirming the shift back to online is structural.

cc-ecom-vol-yoy.title: Ecommerce volume +27.5% YoY — sustained at 4-period high
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card ecommerce transaction volume grew 27.48% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth accelerated 26.12% → 27.48%, the fastest reading in the 4-period series and now at the maximum of the 26.1–27.5% range.
cc-ecom-vol-yoy.inference: Ecommerce transaction frequency is growing at 27% YoY with minimal deceleration — the channel is adding volume at 3.3× the value growth rate, confirming cardholders are transacting more often at lower ticket sizes.

cc-other-val-abs.title: CC other transactions hit ₹90,228 Cr — recovery resumes after Feb pause
cc-other-val-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction value reached ₹90,228 Cr in Mar 2026, up ₹14,520 Cr from Feb 2026.
cc-other-val-abs.direction: Value surged ₹75,708 Cr → ₹90,228 Cr, erasing the prior month's decline and posting the second-largest monthly gain on record — the level is now 6× the Apr 2025 low of ₹15,687 Cr.
cc-other-val-abs.inference: The Feb decline was a one-month pause, not a trend break — the recovery has resumed with force. This new transaction category (likely tap-to-pay, transit, or merchant-initiated flows) is still scaling rapidly despite the maturing base. The trajectory confirms structural embedding, not a ceiling.

cc-other-val-share.title: CC other share holds at 0.04% — still negligible in value mix
cc-other-val-share.observation: Other transactions account for 0.04% of total credit card transaction value in Mar 2026, unchanged from Feb 2026.
cc-other-val-share.direction: Share held flat at 0.04%, the highest reading in the 16-period window but still negligible in the overall mix.
cc-other-val-share.inference: Despite explosive absolute growth, other channels remain strategically irrelevant in value terms — ecommerce and POS continue to dominate credit card spending by orders of magnitude. This category is not yet material for product strategy or revenue forecasting.

cc-other-val-yoy.title: CC other value YoY at 495.9% — still fastest-growing channel
cc-other-val-yoy.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction value grew 495.9% YoY in Mar 2026, down 21.1 points from Feb 2026's 517.0%.
cc-other-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 517.0% → 495.9%, the third consecutive monthly slowdown but still near the top of the four-period range.
cc-other-val-yoy.inference: This remains the fastest-growing credit card channel by a wide margin — a 5× year-on-year surge confirms a new use case is scaling rapidly. The deceleration is expected as the base effect fades, but the absolute rate is still extreme. Lenders should monitor whether this velocity sustains below 450% in coming quarters as the category matures.

cc-other-vol-abs.title: CC other volume at 3.34M transactions — recovery resumes after Feb pause
cc-other-vol-abs.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction volume reached 3.34M in Mar 2026, up 513K from Feb 2026.
cc-other-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 2.83M → 3.34M, erasing the prior month's decline and posting the largest monthly gain on record — the level is now 4× the Apr 2025 low of 854K.
cc-other-vol-abs.inference: The Feb decline was a one-month pause, not a trend break — the recovery has resumed with force. This new transaction type (likely tap-to-pay, transit, or subscriptions) is still embedding rapidly in cardholder behaviour despite the maturing base. The trajectory confirms structural adoption, not a ceiling.

cc-other-vol-share.title: CC other volume share at 0.58% — 11th straight monthly gain
cc-other-vol-share.observation: Other transactions represent 0.58% of total credit card transaction volume in Mar 2026, up 0.01 points from Feb 2026.
cc-other-vol-share.direction: Share rose 0.57% → 0.58%, the highest reading in the 16-period window and the eleventh consecutive monthly gain.
cc-other-vol-share.inference: Other channels are gaining share for the eleventh straight month — this confirms the category is still embedding structurally in cardholder behaviour even as YoY growth decelerates. The share trajectory is decoupling from the absolute level, signaling this use case is becoming a permanent fixture in the transaction mix.

cc-other-vol-yoy.title: CC other volume YoY at 308.4% — still fastest-growing channel
cc-other-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card 'other' transaction volume grew 308.4% YoY in Mar 2026, down 8.8 points from Feb 2026's 317.2%.
cc-other-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated 317.2% → 308.4%, the third consecutive monthly slowdown but still near the top of the four-period range.
cc-other-vol-yoy.inference: This remains the fastest-growing credit card channel by volume — a 4× year-on-year surge confirms a new use case is scaling rapidly. The deceleration is expected as the base effect fades, but the absolute rate is still extreme. Lenders should monitor whether this velocity sustains below 300% in coming quarters as the category matures.

cc-pos-val-abs.title: CC POS value surged to ₹78,470 Cr — up ₹11,035 Cr from Feb
cc-pos-val-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction value reached ₹78,470 Cr in Mar 2026, up ₹11,035 Cr from Feb 2026's ₹67,435 Cr.
cc-pos-val-abs.direction: Value jumped ₹67,435 Cr → ₹78,470 Cr, a 16.4% monthly gain — the strongest single-month recovery in this dataset and the highest level since Dec 2025 (₹78,476 Cr).
cc-pos-val-abs.inference: The two-month POS contraction has reversed sharply — Mar's ₹11,035 Cr surge erases the entire Jan-Feb pullback and confirms the decline was seasonal, not structural. Offline retail demand has snapped back to festive-season levels. Lenders should expect POS volumes to stabilise near ₹78,000 Cr as consumer spending normalises post-year-end correction.

cc-pos-val-share.title: POS share fell to 35.7% — lowest in 16 months despite value surge
cc-pos-val-share.observation: POS transactions captured 35.7% of total credit card value in Mar 2026, down 2.3 points from Feb 2026's 38.0%.
cc-pos-val-share.direction: Share fell 38.0% → 35.7%, the fifth consecutive monthly decline from the Nov 2025 peak of 40.0% — a cumulative 4.3 point drop and the lowest level on record.
cc-pos-val-share.inference: POS value surged in absolute terms but lost share dramatically — ecommerce grew even faster, capturing the majority of Mar spending. The 4.3 point drop from Nov confirms offline retail is structurally losing ground despite strong absolute growth. The anticipated POS share recovery has failed — online channels are compounding faster and now dominate total card spend.

cc-pos-val-yoy.title: POS value YoY re-accelerated to 9.8% — highest in dataset
cc-pos-val-yoy.observation: Credit card POS transaction value grew 9.8% YoY in Mar 2026, the strongest rate in the four-period dataset.
cc-pos-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated 8.5% → 9.8%, rising 1.2 points and reaching the maximum level after hitting the floor in Feb.
cc-pos-val-yoy.inference: The Feb deceleration was a one-month blip — Mar's 9.8% YoY gain confirms POS value growth has stabilised near 10% on a year-on-year basis. Offline retail is growing steadily but remains slower than ecommerce, which is why POS share continues to fall despite positive absolute growth.

cc-pos-vol-abs.title: POS volume hit 28.4 Cr transactions — all-time high
cc-pos-vol-abs.observation: Credit card POS transaction volume reached 28.4 Cr in Mar 2026, up 4.1 Cr from Feb 2026's 24.3 Cr.
cc-pos-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 24.3 Cr → 28.4 Cr, a 16.8% monthly gain — the strongest single-month increase in this dataset and the highest level on record, surpassing the Dec 2025 peak of 27.3 Cr.
cc-pos-vol-abs.inference: The two-month volume contraction has reversed decisively — Mar's 4.1 Cr surge sets a new all-time high and confirms the Jan-Feb pullback was seasonal noise. Offline retail transaction counts are now above festive-season levels, signaling sustained consumer engagement at physical points of sale.

cc-pos-vol-share.title: POS volume share fell to 49.1% — below 50% for third straight month
cc-pos-vol-share.observation: POS transactions accounted for 49.1% of total credit card transaction volume in Mar 2026, down 0.1 points from Feb 2026's 49.2%.
cc-pos-vol-share.direction: Share fell 49.2% → 49.1%, the third consecutive month below 50% and the lowest level on record, extending the decline from the Jan 2025 peak of 50.5%.
cc-pos-vol-share.inference: The 50/50 channel equilibrium has definitively broken — POS has now ceded majority share to ecommerce for three straight months despite hitting all-time volume highs. The new structural reality is a sub-50% POS share, confirming online channels are growing faster in transaction count terms. Lenders should plan for a gradual shift toward online-dominant channel mix.

cc-pos-vol-yoy.title: POS volume YoY surged to 23.4% — highest in dataset
cc-pos-vol-yoy.observation: Credit card POS transaction volume grew 23.4% YoY in Mar 2026, the strongest rate in the four-period dataset.
cc-pos-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth re-accelerated 21.7% → 23.4%, rising 1.7 points and reaching the maximum level after decelerating in Feb.
cc-pos-vol-yoy.inference: POS volume growth has re-accelerated to the highest year-on-year rate on record — 23.4% YoY confirms offline retail transaction counts are expanding robustly. However, the simultaneous share loss to ecommerce signals online channels are growing even faster, likely above 25% YoY.

debit_card_txn.narrative: Debit card cash withdrawals at POS terminals are in terminal decline with no evidence of recovery. Value stabilised at ₹17.0M in Mar 2026 — up 2% month-on-month but still 39% below the Jan 2025 peak — while transaction volume fell 7% to 9,984, the second-lowest level on record. Year-on-year, value is down 18% and volume down 48%, with the volume decline re-accelerating after a brief moderation. The channel has never registered above 0.00% of total debit card flows in either value or volume terms across 16 months. Issuers should treat this as a sunset feature with no strategic relevance — the Jan 2025 spike was a one-time anomaly, and the structural floor is now established near current levels.

dc-atm-val-abs.title: ATM cash withdrawals ₹2.48L Cr — recovery from record low
dc-atm-val-abs.observation: Debit card ATM cash withdrawals totaled ₹2,484,190,349 thousands (₹2.48 lakh crore) in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-val-abs.direction: Value surged ₹2.30L Cr → ₹2.48L Cr, gaining ₹18,177 crore — the sharpest monthly recovery in the 16-month window, reversing the prior period's record low.
dc-atm-val-abs.inference: The floor break was temporary — cash withdrawal demand has snapped back above ₹2.40L Cr. The ₹2.34L–₹2.50L Cr oscillation range is intact. ATM network rationalization should pause; the structural collapse thesis is invalidated.

dc-atm-val-share.title: ATM cash share 86.75% — down 0.67pp from record high
dc-atm-val-share.observation: ATM withdrawals accounted for 86.75% of total debit card transaction value in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-val-share.direction: Share fell 87.4% → 86.7%, dropping 0.67pp from the prior period's all-time high and returning to the median range.
dc-atm-val-share.inference: The 87%+ breakout was a one-period spike, not a trend reversal. Cash is not reclaiming structural share from digital channels — the 86%–87% equilibrium holds. Digital channel investment assumptions remain valid.

dc-atm-val-yoy.title: ATM value -5.38% YoY — contraction re-accelerated sharply
dc-atm-val-yoy.observation: ATM withdrawal value declined 5.38% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-val-yoy.direction: Contraction deepened -1.8% → -5.4% YoY, deteriorating 3.60pp and returning to the weakest level in the 4-period window.
dc-atm-val-yoy.inference: The stabilization narrative has collapsed — the -1.8% reading was an outlier, not a trend break. Cash withdrawal demand is contracting at the fastest rate on record. The structural decline thesis is confirmed.

dc-atm-vol-abs.title: ATM withdrawals 444M transactions — recovery from record low
dc-atm-vol-abs.observation: Debit card ATM withdrawals totaled 443,999,321 transactions in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 415M → 444M transactions, gaining 29.5M — the sharpest monthly recovery in the 16-month window, reversing the prior period's record low.
dc-atm-vol-abs.inference: The floor break was temporary — visit frequency has snapped back to the median range. The 435M–465M oscillation range is intact. ATM network rationalization should pause; the structural collapse thesis is invalidated.

dc-atm-vol-share.title: ATM volume share 81.42% — down 0.46pp from record high
dc-atm-vol-share.observation: ATM withdrawals represented 81.42% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-vol-share.direction: Share fell 81.9% → 81.4%, dropping 0.46pp from the prior period's all-time high and returning to the upper range.
dc-atm-vol-share.inference: The 81.9% spike was a one-period outlier, not a structural shift. ATM visit share is oscillating in the 80%–82% range, not breaking out. Digital channels are not losing transaction count on a sustained basis.

dc-atm-vol-yoy.title: ATM volume -10.43% YoY — worst contraction on record
dc-atm-vol-yoy.observation: ATM withdrawal volume declined 10.43% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-atm-vol-yoy.direction: Contraction deepened -7.4% → -10.4% YoY, deteriorating 3.09pp and reaching the worst level in the 4-period window.
dc-atm-vol-yoy.inference: The stabilization narrative has collapsed — the -7.4% reading was an outlier, not a trend break. Visit frequency is contracting at the fastest rate on record. The structural decline thesis is confirmed.

dc-ecom-val-abs.title: Ecommerce value ₹13,434 Cr — recovery from record low
dc-ecom-val-abs.observation: Debit card ecommerce transactions totaled ₹134,339,503 thousands (₹13,434 crore) in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-val-abs.direction: Value surged ₹11,110 Cr → ₹13,434 Cr, gaining ₹2,324 crore — the sharpest monthly recovery in the 16-month window, reversing the prior period's record low.
dc-ecom-val-abs.inference: The floor break was temporary — digital commerce value has snapped back above ₹13,000 Cr and into the upper range. The ₹11,000–₹14,000 Cr oscillation range is intact. Ecommerce partnerships should not be exited.

dc-ecom-val-share.title: Ecommerce share 4.69% — up 0.47pp from floor
dc-ecom-val-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions accounted for 4.69% of total debit card transaction value in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-val-share.direction: Share rose 4.22% → 4.69%, gaining 0.47pp and moving to the upper range of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-val-share.inference: Digital commerce is regaining value share — the 4.22% floor was a temporary dip. The 4.7% level is near the historical upper range. Ecommerce is oscillating in the 4.2%–5.2% band with upward bias.

dc-ecom-val-yoy.title: Ecommerce value +2.73% YoY — growth accelerating
dc-ecom-val-yoy.observation: Ecommerce transaction value grew 2.73% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-val-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated +1.4% → +2.7% YoY, improving 1.29pp and moving to the median of the 4-period window.
dc-ecom-val-yoy.inference: The recovery is gaining momentum — ecommerce value is growing at an accelerating rate. The structural contraction narrative is fully invalidated. If growth crosses 5% next period, the breakout is confirmed.

dc-ecom-vol-abs.title: Ecommerce volume 26.3M transactions — recovery from record low
dc-ecom-vol-abs.observation: Debit card ecommerce transactions totaled 26,307,005 in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.direction: Volume surged 22.7M → 26.3M transactions, gaining 3.6M — the sharpest monthly recovery in the 16-month window, reversing the prior period's record low.
dc-ecom-vol-abs.inference: The floor break was temporary — transaction count has snapped back to the median range. Adoption is not structurally contracting; it is oscillating. Ecommerce partnerships should not be exited based on the prior period's collapse.

dc-ecom-vol-share.title: Ecommerce volume share 4.82% — up 0.35pp from floor
dc-ecom-vol-share.observation: Ecommerce transactions represented 4.82% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-share.direction: Share rose 4.48% → 4.82%, gaining 0.35pp and moving to the median of the 16-month window.
dc-ecom-vol-share.inference: Volume share is recovering from the floor — ecommerce is regaining transaction share. The 4.5% floor was a temporary dip. The 4.8% level is near the historical median. Banks should expect ecommerce to oscillate in the 4.5%–5.0% range.

dc-ecom-vol-yoy.title: Ecommerce volume -14.09% YoY — contraction moderating
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.observation: Ecommerce transaction volume declined 14.09% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.direction: Contraction moderated -18.5% → -14.1% YoY, improving 4.43pp and moving to the median of the 4-period window.
dc-ecom-vol-yoy.inference: Visit frequency contraction is easing at an accelerating pace — the -14.1% decline is 4.4pp better than the prior period. The structural collapse narrative is weakening. If the next period improves further, the trend break is confirmed.

dc-other-val-abs.title: DC other value ₹168 Cr — up ₹5.1 Cr, above p75
dc-other-val-abs.observation: Debit card 'other' transactions totaled ₹1,679,903 thousands (₹168.0 crore) in Mar 2026.
dc-other-val-abs.direction: Value rose ₹162.9 Cr → ₹168.0 Cr, up ₹5.1 crore and now sitting above the 75th percentile of the 16-month range.
dc-other-val-abs.inference: The Feb dip was noise — value has climbed back to the upper end of its historical range. However, absolute scale remains negligible (under 0.1% of total debit card value), so this uptick carries no strategic weight for portfolio allocation.

dc-other-val-share.title: DC other share flat at 0.06% — no structural shift
dc-other-val-share.observation: Other transactions accounted for 0.06% of total debit card transaction value in Mar 2026.
dc-other-val-share.direction: Share held flat at 0.06%, unchanged from Feb 2026 and locked near the median of the 16-month window.
dc-other-val-share.inference: No new use case is emerging. This category remains structurally irrelevant — banks should continue to exclude it from strategic planning and resource allocation.

dc-other-val-yoy.title: DC other value YoY +5.5% — growth decelerating sharply
dc-other-val-yoy.observation: Other transaction value grew 5.47% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-other-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth decelerated sharply: +12.4% → +5.5%, down 7.0 points and now sitting near the median of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-other-val-yoy.inference: The Feb spike was a one-period anomaly — growth has reverted to mid-single digits. Given the negligible absolute base, this volatility is noise. Banks should not allocate resources to this segment.

dc-other-vol-abs.title: DC other volume 78,017 txns — up 4,087, near median
dc-other-vol-abs.observation: Debit card 'other' transactions totaled 78,017 in Mar 2026.
dc-other-vol-abs.direction: Volume rose 73,930 → 78,017 transactions, up 4,087 and now sitting near the median of the 16-month range.
dc-other-vol-abs.inference: Transaction count has recovered from the Feb low, but remains structurally small. Absolute numbers are too low to influence debit card strategy — banks should not allocate resources to this category.

dc-other-vol-share.title: DC other volume share 0.01% — structurally capped at zero
dc-other-vol-share.observation: Other transactions represented 0.01% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026.
dc-other-vol-share.direction: Share held flat at 0.01% (rounded to 0.0%), unchanged from Feb 2026 and structurally capped near zero across the 16-month window.
dc-other-vol-share.inference: Volume share remains structurally irrelevant. This category will not move the needle on debit card strategy — banks should ignore it for resource allocation.

dc-other-vol-yoy.title: DC other volume -20.3% YoY — contraction moderating slightly
dc-other-vol-yoy.observation: Other transaction volume contracted 20.34% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-other-vol-yoy.direction: YoY decline moderated: -21.4% → -20.3%, improving 1.0 point and now sitting near the median of the 4-period YoY window.
dc-other-vol-yoy.inference: The category is shrinking less sharply than in Feb, but remains in structural decline. No growth driver exists — banks should not allocate resources to this segment.

dc-pos-val-abs.title: DC POS value rebounds to ₹243.5B — up ₹24.8B from Feb low
dc-pos-val-abs.observation: Debit card POS transaction value rose to ₹243.5 billion in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-val-abs.direction: Value climbed from ₹218.6B in Feb 2026 to ₹243.5B in Mar 2026 — a ₹24.8B recovery (11.4% month-on-month) — moving back toward the historical median after hitting a 16-month low last period.
dc-pos-val-abs.inference: The Feb collapse was a one-month anomaly, not a structural break. POS value has snapped back to the ₹240B–₹260B range that held for most of 2025. The channel has stabilised, but remains range-bound with no breakout momentum.

dc-pos-val-share.title: POS share edges up to 8.50% — still near 16-month low
dc-pos-val-share.observation: POS transactions accounted for 8.50% of total debit card transaction value in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-val-share.direction: Share rose from 8.30% in Feb 2026 to 8.50% in Mar 2026 — a 0.20 percentage point gain — but remains below the 8.5%–9.1% band that held for most of the prior year.
dc-pos-val-share.inference: The Feb share collapse has partially reversed, but POS is still at the bottom of its historical range. The channel is not regaining structural share — it is treading water while other channels (likely ATM cash) hold a larger slice of total debit card value.

dc-pos-val-yoy.title: POS value -5.70% YoY — contraction unchanged from Feb
dc-pos-val-yoy.observation: POS transaction value contracted 5.70% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-val-yoy.direction: YoY growth held steady at -5.83% in Feb 2026 and -5.70% in Mar 2026 — a marginal 0.13 percentage point improvement — with the channel now contracting for four consecutive months.
dc-pos-val-yoy.inference: The YoY decline has plateaued at -6%, but this is not a recovery — it is a sustained contraction. The month-on-month rebound in absolute value has not translated into year-on-year momentum. POS remains in a structural decline phase.

dc-pos-vol-abs.title: POS volume rises to 74.9M — up 6.0M from Feb trough
dc-pos-vol-abs.observation: Debit card POS transaction volume rose to 74.9 million in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-vol-abs.direction: Volume climbed from 69.0M in Feb 2026 to 74.9M in Mar 2026 — a 6.0M gain (8.6% month-on-month) — but remains below the 76M–84M range that held for five months through Jan 2026.
dc-pos-vol-abs.inference: Transaction frequency has partially recovered from the Feb collapse, but remains 18% below the 91M Jan 2025 baseline. Debit cardholders are using POS more than in Feb, but usage intensity has not returned to prior norms. The channel is stabilising at a lower equilibrium.

dc-pos-vol-share.title: POS volume share 13.74% — still at 16-month low
dc-pos-vol-share.observation: POS transactions represented 13.74% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-vol-share.direction: Share rose marginally from 13.63% in Feb 2026 to 13.74% in Mar 2026 — a 0.12 percentage point gain — but remains below the 14.2%–14.9% band that held for seven months through Jan 2026.
dc-pos-vol-share.inference: POS has regained a sliver of frequency share, but is still at the bottom of its historical range. The channel is not reclaiming its footprint — it is holding steady at a structurally lower share of debit card transaction volume.

dc-pos-vol-yoy.title: POS volume -16.35% YoY — contraction deepens 2.2pp from Feb
dc-pos-vol-yoy.observation: POS transaction volume contracted 16.35% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-vol-yoy.direction: YoY growth deteriorated from -14.15% in Feb 2026 to -16.35% in Mar 2026 — a 2.21 percentage point worsening — reversing the marginal improvement seen last period.
dc-pos-vol-yoy.inference: The YoY decline has re-accelerated despite the month-on-month volume rebound. The Mar 2025 base was stronger, exposing the structural weakness in current usage. POS transaction frequency is contracting at the fastest rate in the 4-period window — the channel is losing users, not just value per transaction.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.title: DC POS cash withdrawals at ₹17.0M — up 2% MoM
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.observation: Debit card cash withdrawals at POS terminals totaled ₹17.0 million in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.direction: Value rose from ₹16.7M in Feb 2026 to ₹17.0M in Mar 2026 — up 2% month-on-month, the first sequential gain in three months.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-abs.inference: The Feb 2026 decline has reversed, but the ₹17.0M level remains 39% below the Jan 2025 peak of ₹27.7M and near the structural floor established in mid-2025. This is a stabilisation bounce, not a recovery — the channel remains in terminal decline with no evidence of sustained demand.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.title: DC POS cash withdrawal share flat at 0.00%
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals accounted for 0.00% of total debit card transaction value in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.direction: Share held at 0.00% across all 16 periods, unchanged from Feb 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-share.inference: Despite the month-on-month value recovery, cash-at-POS has never registered in the debit card value mix. The feature is economically irrelevant — not a strategic consideration for issuers, acquirers, or network operators.

dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.title: DC POS cash withdrawal value -17.9% YoY — decline moderating
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.observation: POS cash withdrawal value contracted 17.9% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.direction: YoY decline improved from -21.6% in Feb 2026 to -17.9% in Mar 2026 — the least severe contraction in the 4-period YoY window, continuing the moderation trend from -31.3% in Jan 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-val-yoy.inference: The year-on-year comparison is improving for the third straight month, but only because the Mar 2025 base was already depressed at ₹20.7M. The channel remains structurally down one-fifth versus prior year — moderation in the rate of decline does not signal recovery, merely a lower base effect as the Jan 2025 spike rolls off.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume at 9,984 — down 7% MoM
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.observation: Debit card cash withdrawal transactions at POS terminals reached 9,984 in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.direction: Volume dropped from 10,737 in Feb 2026 to 9,984 in Mar 2026 — down 7% month-on-month, falling below the 10,000 threshold for the first time since Sep 2025 and now in the lower quartile of the 16-period range.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-abs.inference: The Feb 2026 decline has extended — transaction count is now at its second-lowest level on record, 59% below the Jan 2025 peak of 24,327. The channel is in terminal decline with no evidence of stabilisation. Issuers should complete sunset plans immediately.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume share flat at 0.00%
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.observation: POS cash withdrawals represented 0.00% of total debit card transaction volume in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.direction: Share held at 0.00% across all 16 periods, unchanged from Feb 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-share.inference: Despite the 59% volume collapse from Jan 2025 to Mar 2026, cash-at-POS remains a rounding error in the debit card transaction mix. The feature has never scaled and shows no sign of registering in the overall volume distribution — not a material driver of debit card strategy.

dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.title: DC POS cash withdrawal volume -47.7% YoY — decline re-accelerating
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.observation: POS cash withdrawal transaction volume contracted 47.7% year-on-year in Mar 2026.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.direction: YoY decline worsened from -44.3% in Feb 2026 to -47.7% in Mar 2026 — reversing the prior month's moderation and returning near the Jan 2026 low of -49.2%.
dc-pos-withdrawal-vol-yoy.inference: The year-on-year comparison confirms terminal trajectory: Mar 2026 volume is down 48% versus Mar 2025, despite the recent month-on-month volatility. The Jan 2025 spike is now definitively confirmed as a one-time anomaly. This channel has no strategic future — issuers should accelerate sunset timelines.
